From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

19th United States national census

| Nineteenth census of the United States | |
| --- | --- |
| ---   ← [1960](/wiki/1960_United_States_census "1960 United States census")  **April 1, 1970**  [1980](/wiki/1980_United_States_census "1980 United States census") →   --- | |
| U.S. Census Bureau seal | |
| 1970 U.S. census logo | |
| General information | |
| Country | United States |
| Results | |
| Total population | 203,392,031 (Increase 13.4%) |
| Most populous state | [California](/wiki/California "California") 19,953,134 |
| Least populous state | [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") 302,173 |

The **1970 United States census**, conducted by the [Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), determined the resident population of the United States to be 203,392,031, an increase of 13.4 percent over the 179,323,175 persons [enumerated](/wiki/Enumeration "Enumeration") during the [1960 census](/wiki/1960_United_States_census "1960 United States census").

This was the first census since 1820 in which [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") was not the most populous state—[California](/wiki/California "California") overtook it in population in January 1963. This was also the first census in which all states recorded a population of over 300,000, and the first in which a city in the geographic South—[Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston")—recorded a population of over 1 million.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Population_change_recorded_by_the_1970_Census_by_State.png/250px-Population_change_recorded_by_the_1970_Census_by_State.png)](/wiki/File:Population_change_recorded_by_the_1970_Census_by_State.png)

Percentage population change by state since the previous census (1960)

Accurate racial census data was required by both the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964") and the [Voting Rights Act of 1965](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965"), and the 1970 Census changed to identify race on the basis of self-identification rather than by government census enumerators.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Virtually all of the key items from the 1960 census were retained.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Data availability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970_United_States_census&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Data availability")]

[Microdata](/wiki/Microdata_(statistics) "Microdata (statistics)") from the 1970 census are freely available through the [Integrated Public Use Microdata Series](/wiki/Integrated_Public_Use_Microdata_Series "Integrated Public Use Microdata Series"). [Aggregate data](/wiki/Aggregate_data "Aggregate data") for small areas, together with electronic boundary files, can be downloaded from the [National Historical Geographic Information System](/wiki/National_Historical_Geographic_Information_System "National Historical Geographic Information System"). These data were originally created and disseminated by [DUALabs](/wiki/DUALabs "DUALabs"). Personally identifiable information will be available in 2042.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Conclusions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970_United_States_census&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Conclusions")]

California took over as the most populous state; New York had previously been ranked number one. While the entire country increased to more than 204 million persons, four states lost population, with West Virginia leading the list, down roughly 7 percent from 1960.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

On April 25, 1973, the Census Bureau announced the results of a reanalysis of the 1970 census. The report estimated that the census undercounted approximately 5.33 million people, for a net undercount rate of 2.5%. This would have made the population approximately 208.7 million. Of those undercounted, an estimated 3.45 million were white, and 1.88 million were black.[[7]](#cite_note-7)
